evals.name: document-management-mcp-schemas
evals.prompt: I'm building an MCP server for a document management system. I need tools for uploading documents, searching by content, and downloading. Design the tool schemas (names, descriptions, parameters) for these three tools. Include the full JSON schema for each tool's parameters and write the description field so an LLM agent will invoke each tool correctly.
evals.expected_output: Three well-designed MCP tool schemas with: (1) names following verb_noun convention, (2) descriptions written as 'when to use' instructions (not 'what it does'), (3) parameter descriptions with concrete examples, (4) boundary/exclusion clauses in descriptions, (5) return value documentation. The descriptions should help an LLM agent distinguish between search vs download vs upload without confusion.
expectations: Tool descriptions use 'when to use' framing rather than 'what it does' framing
Parameter descriptions include concrete format examples (not just type annotations)
Each tool description includes boundary/exclusion clauses stating what the tool does NOT accept
Return value structure is documented for at least one tool
Tool names follow a consistent verb_noun or action-oriented convention
The search tool description distinguishes it from the download tool to prevent agent confusion

evals.name: search-vs-read-tool-confusion
evals.prompt: My agent keeps calling the wrong tool — it uses `search_files` when it should use `read_file`. Both tools exist. Here are their current descriptions: search_files: 'Search for files', read_file: 'Read a file'. What's wrong and how do I fix it? Give me the rewritten descriptions.
evals.expected_output: Diagnosis that the descriptions are too vague and don't specify 'when to use' each tool. Rewritten descriptions that: (1) specify the decision condition for each tool, (2) include boundary clauses (search_files does NOT read content; read_file requires knowing the exact path), (3) are distinct enough that an agent mid-task can unambiguously choose the right one. Ideally includes the principle that descriptions are instructions, not labels.
expectations: Identifies that the root cause is vague 'what it does' descriptions rather than model failure
Rewrites search_files description with a 'when to use' clause (e.g., 'Use when you need to find files by name or pattern but don't know the exact path')
Rewrites read_file description with a 'when to use' clause (e.g., 'Use when you already know the exact file path and need its contents')
At least one rewritten description includes a boundary/exclusion clause
The two rewritten descriptions are clearly distinguishable — an agent reading both would know which to call

evals.name: jira-tool-proliferation-consolidation
evals.prompt: I have 15 separate API tools for different Jira operations: create_issue, update_issue, delete_issue, list_issues, add_comment, update_comment, delete_comment, list_comments, get_issue, get_project, list_projects, assign_issue, set_priority, add_label, remove_label. My agent is struggling to pick the right one. Should I consolidate these? How? Give me a concrete consolidation plan.
evals.expected_output: Analysis using granularity principles: identifies that 15 tools is approaching the ~100-tool confusion threshold and that many of these tools share the same logical object. Concrete consolidation plan that groups by: (1) CRUD on issues (create/read/update/delete), (2) CRUD on comments, (3) project operations, (4) issue metadata (assign/priority/labels). Recommends merging with an 'operation' parameter pattern. Explains the merge signal criteria (same logical object, always called in sequence, distinguishing requires domain knowledge). Final count should be 4-6 tools.
expectations: Applies the granularity principle: tools that operate on the same logical object are candidates for merging
Identifies the specific merge signal: distinguishing between these tools requires domain knowledge the model shouldn't need
Proposes a concrete consolidation (not just 'consider merging') with specific tool groupings
The proposed consolidated tool count is meaningfully lower (ideally 4-6 tools from 15)
At least one consolidated tool schema or description is provided as an example
Mentions or applies the ~100-tool selection-accuracy threshold or equivalent reasoning about tool proliferation
